name: Express.js
what_it_does: Minimal and unopinionated Node.js web framework that provides HTTP routing, middleware composition, and request/response handling. The de facto standard for Node.js APIs. Agents use Express to build API servers or microservices. No automatic OpenAPI generation (requires swagger-jsdoc or tsoa), no built-in validation — add via middleware.
use_cases: Building REST API backends that agents or other services consume
Creating lightweight microservices and HTTP endpoints in Node.js
Rapid API prototyping where middleware flexibility matters
Serving agent tool endpoints in JavaScript/TypeScript ecosystems
Building proxies and middleware layers between services
not_for: Teams that need built-in TypeScript support, validation, or OpenAPI (use Fastify or NestJS)
Full-stack applications needing templating, ORM, auth out of the box (use Next.js or Nest.js)
High-performance APIs under heavy load where Fastify's lower overhead matters significantly
best_when: You need a simple, flexible Node.js HTTP server with minimal opinions, and your team is comfortable assembling middleware manually. Great for quick microservices and agent tool endpoints in a JavaScript ecosystem.
avoid_when: You need automatic OpenAPI spec generation, strong TypeScript types, built-in validation, or a more opinionated structure — use NestJS, Fastify, or Hono instead.

auth.notes: No built-in auth — everything via middleware. Passport.js is the standard auth middleware ecosystem with 500+ strategies. express-jwt or jsonwebtoken for JWT validation. API key auth via custom middleware. CORS via cors package. Auth is entirely the developer's responsibility — no framework-level enforcement.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free and open source (MIT). No usage limits.
pricing.notes: Express is MIT-licensed and has been in maintenance mode (4.x) for years with version 5 in long-running release cycle. The project is community-maintained under the expressjs GitHub org. Zero cost, no vendor relationship.

performance.rate_limits: No built-in rate limiting. Add express-rate-limit middleware. Performance is solid for most workloads; Fastify is benchmarked ~2x faster for high-throughput APIs.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Express has no built-in error format — error handling is entirely custom. Without an error handler middleware, unhandled errors return an HTML error page, not JSON, which breaks agent parsing. Developers must implement a JSON error handler middleware explicitly. The ecosystem pattern (using http-errors + express-validator) provides good structure, but it is not automatic.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No framework-level idempotency support. Idempotency is entirely the application developer's responsibility. No built-in idempotency key handling.
known_agent_gotchas: No built-in JSON error format — agents consuming Express APIs may receive HTML error pages on 500 errors if the developer didn't add a JSON error handler
No automatic OpenAPI spec — agents cannot dynamically discover Express API endpoints without separately maintained documentation
No built-in request validation — malformed requests may reach handler code and produce unpredictable errors
Express 4.x does not handle async errors automatically — unhandled promise rejections in route handlers won't be caught by error middleware (fixed in Express 5.x)
Middleware ordering is critical and invisible to API consumers — auth, validation, and parsing order must be correct
express.json() body parser must be explicitly added — missing it causes 'req.body is undefined' which is a common source of silent failures

security_score_components.security_notes: Security is entirely opt-in via middleware. Helmet.js for HTTP security headers (not included by default). No CSRF protection by default. No input validation by default — add express-validator or Joi. Express 4.x has numerous dependencies with periodic CVEs. HTTPS must be handled by the deployment environment (nginx, ALB). The framework is intentionally minimal — security posture depends almost entirely on developer choices.
